elements.sent-2411-001: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have substantially advanced atomistic simulations in materials science and chemistry by providing a compelling balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-002: While leading MLIPs rely on representations of atomic environments using spherical tensors, Cartesian representations offer potential advantages in simplicity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we introduce Cartesian Atomic Moment Potentials (CAMP), an approach equivalent to models based on spherical tensors but operating entirely in the Cartesian space.
elements.sent-2411-004: CAMP constructs atomic moment tensors from neighboring atoms and combines these through tensor products to incorporate higher body-order interactions, which can provide a complete description of local atomic environments.
elements.sent-2411-005: By integrating these into a graph neural network (GNN) framework, CAMP enables physically-motivated and systematically improvable potentials.
elements.sent-2411-006: It requires minimal hyperparameter tuning that simplifies the training process.
elements.sent-2411-007: The model demonstrates excellent performance across diverse systems, including periodic structures, small organic molecules, and two-dimensional materials.
elements.sent-2411-008: It achieves accuracy, efficiency, and stability in molecular dynamics simulations surpassing or comparable to current leading models.
elements.sent-2411-009: By combining the strengths of Cartesian representations with the expressiveness of GNNs, CAMP provides a powerful tool for atomistic simulations to accelerate materials understanding and discovery.
elements.sent-2411-010: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have been widely employed in molecular simulations to investigate the properties of all kinds of materials, ranging from small organic molecules to inorganic crystals and biological systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: MLIPs offer a balance between accuracy and efficiency~\cite{huang2021initio, unke2021machine, deringer2021gaussian, wen2021kliff , making them a suitable choice standing between first-principles methods such as density functional theory (DFT) and classical interatomic potentials such as the embedded atom method (EAM)~\cite{daw1984embedded .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Since the introduction of the pioneering Behler--Parrinello neural network (BPNN) potential~\cite{behler2007generalized and the Gaussian approximation potential (GAP)~\cite{bartok2010gaussian , many valuable MLIPs have been developed~\cite{chmiela2017machine, zhang2018deep, schutt2018schnet, zubatyuk2019accurate, gasteiger2020dimenet, schutt2021equivariant, ko2021fourth, unke2021spookynet, batatia2022mace, batzner2022e, takamoto2022teanet, simeon2023tensornet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: At the same time, techniques have emerged to assess the reliability of MLIP predictions by quantifying uncertainties and evaluating their influence on downstream properties~\cite{wen2020uncertainty, zhu2023fast, tan2023single, vita2024ltau, dai2024uncertainty .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite this progress, the pursuit of enhancing and developing new MLIPs remains ongoing.
elements.sent-2411-015: Current leading MLIPs in terms of accuracy and stability in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are those based on graph neural networks (GNNs).
elements.sent-2411-016: These models represent an atomic structure as a graph and perform message passing between atoms to propagate information \cite{gilmer2017neural .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The passed messages can be either scalars \cite{schutt2018schnet,gasteiger2020dimenet , vectors \cite{schutt2021equivariant , or, more generally, tensors \cite{batzner2022e, takamoto2022teanet, simeon2023tensornet, batatia2022mace .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Using GNNs, a couple of universal MLIPs have recently been developed \cite{chen2022a, takamoto2022towards, deng2023chgnet, batatia2023a, xie2024gptff, zhang2023dpa, yang2024mattersim , which can cover a wide range of materials with chemical species across the periodic table.}
elements.sent-2411-019: At the core of any MLIP lies a description of the atomic environment, which converts the information contained in an atomic neighborhood to a numerical representation that can then be updated and used in an ML regression algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-020: The representation must satisfy certain symmetries (invariance to permutation of atoms, and invariance or equivariance to translation, rotation, and inversion) inherent to atomic systems.
elements.sent-2411-021: Despite the diverse choice of ML regression algorithms, the atomic environment representation is typically constructed by expanding atomic positions on some basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-022: Early works employ manually crafted basis functions for the bond distances and bond angles, such as the atom-centered symmetry functions used in BPNN \cite{behler2007generalized and ANI-1 \cite{smith2017ani .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: More systematic representations have been developed and adopted in GAP \cite{bartok2010gaussian , SNAP \cite{thompson2015spectral , ACE \cite{drautz2019atomic , NequIP \cite{batzner2022e , and MACE \cite{batatia2022mace , among others.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In these models, a representation is obtained by first expanding the atomic environment using a radial basis (e.g., Chebyshev polynomials) and the spherical harmonics angular basis to obtain tensorial equivariant features.
elements.sent-2411-025: The equivariant features are then updated and finally contracted to yield the scalar potential energy.
elements.sent-2411-026: Another approach seeks to develop atomic representations and MLIPs entirely in Cartesian space, bypassing the need for spherical harmonics.
elements.sent-2411-027: This can potentially offer greater simplicity and reduced complexity compared to models using spherical representations.
elements.sent-2411-028: The MTP model by Shapeev \cite{shapeev2016moment adopted this approach, which constructs Cartesian moment tensors to capture the angular information of an atomic environment.}
elements.sent-2411-029: It has been shown that MTP and ACE are highly related given that a moment tensor can be expressed using spherical harmonics~\cite{dusson2022atomic .}
elements.sent-2411-030: TeaNet~\cite{takamoto2022teanet and TensorNet~\cite{simeon2023tensornet have explored using Cartesian tensors limited to second rank to create MLIPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The recent CACE model \cite{cheng2024cartesian has extended the atomic cluster expansion to Cartesian space and subsequently built MLIPs on this foundation.}
elements.sent-2411-032: These works represent excellent efforts toward building MLIPs using a fully Cartesian representation of atomic environments.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, their performance has not yet matched that of models built using spherical tensors.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this work, we propose Cartesian Atomic Moment Potential (\model), a systematically improvable MLIP developed in the Cartesian space under the GNN framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: Inspired by MTP~\cite{shapeev2016moment , \model employs physically motivated moment tensors to characterize atomic environments.}
elements.sent-2411-036: It then generates hyper moments through tensor products of these moment tensors, effectively capturing higher-order interactions.
elements.sent-2411-037: We have devised specific construction rules for atomic and hyper moment tensors that direct message flow from higher-rank to lower-rank tensors, ultimately to scalars.
elements.sent-2411-038: This approach aligns with our objective of modeling potential energy (a scalar quantity) and significantly reduces the number of moment tensors, enhancing computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-039: These moment tensors are subsequently integrated into a message-passing GNN framework, undergoing iterative updates and refinement to form the final \model.
elements.sent-2411-040: To evaluate the model's performance, we have conducted tests across diverse material systems, including periodic LiPS inorganic crystals~\cite{batzner2022e and bulk water~\cite{cheng2019ab , non-periodic small organic molecules~\cite{chmiela2017machine , and partially periodic two-dimensional (2D) graphene~\cite{wen2019hybrid .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: These comprehensive benchmarking studies demonstrate that \model is an accurate, efficient, and stable MLIP for molecular simulations.
elements.sent-2411-042: We discuss the key components and model design choices below.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{model_architecture.pdf} \caption{Schematic overview of the \model model architecture. a An atomic structure is represented as a graph. Each atom is associated with a feature $\bm h$, and all atoms within a distance of $r_\text{cut}$ to a center atom $i$ constitute its local atomic neighborhood. b Atomic numbers $z_i$ and $z_j$ of a pair of atoms and their relative position vector $\bm r$ are expanded into the radial part $\bm R$ and angular part $\bm D$. c Atomic moment $\bm M$ of the central atom is obtained by aggregating information from neighboring atoms. d Hyper moment $\bm H$ of the central atom is computed by self-interactions between atomic moments. e Hyper moments $\bm H^t$ of different layers $t$ are used to construct the energy of the central atom via some mapping function $V$. } \label{fig:model:arch}
elements.sent-2411-043: An atomic structure is represented as a graph $G(V, E)$ consisting of a set of nodes $V$ and a set of edges $E$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Each node in $V$ represents an atom, and an edge in $E$ is created between two atoms if they are within a cutoff distance of $r_\mathrm{cut}$ (\fref{fig:model:arch a).}
elements.sent-2411-045: An atom node $i$ is described by a 3-tuple $(\bm r^i, z^i, \bm h^i)$, where $\bm r^i$ is the position of the atom, $z^i$ is the atomic number, and $\bm h^i$ is the atom feature.
elements.sent-2411-046: The vector $\bm r^{ij = \bm r^j - \bm r^i$ associated with the edge from atom $i$ to atom $j$ gives their relative position.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The atom feature $\bm h^i$ is a set of tensors that carry two indices $u$ and $v$, becoming $\bm h^i_{uv}$ in its full form, where $u$ and $v$ denote the channel and rank of each feature tensor, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-048: The use of channels allows for multiple copies of tensors of the same rank, helping to increase the expressiveness of the feature.
elements.sent-2411-049: The atom features are mixed across different channels using a linear mapping: \begin{equation}\bm h^i_{uv} = \sum_{u'} W_{uu'} \bm h^i_{u'v},\end{equation} where $W_{uu'}$ are trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-050: The edge length $r^{ij = \|\bm r^{ij}\|$ is expanded using a set of radial basis functions, $B_u(r^{ij , z^i, z^j)$, indexed by the channel $u$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Following MTP \cite{shapeev2016moment, novikov2020mlip , each basis function is a linear combination of the Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind $Q^\beta$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:radial:basis} B_u(r^{ij}, z^i, z^j) = \sum_{\beta=0}^{N_\beta} W_{u z^i z^j}^\beta Q^\beta \left(\frac{r^{ij}}{r_\mathrm{cut}} \right),\end{equation} where $N_\beta$ denotes the maximum degree of the Chebyshev polynomials.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:radial:basis} B_u(r^{ij}, z^i, z^j) = \sum_{\beta=0}^{N_\beta} W_{u z^i z^j}^\beta Q^\beta \left(\frac{r^{ij}}{r_cut} \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: Separate trainable parameters $W_{u z^i z^j ^\beta$ are used for different atom pairs, enabling customized radial basis dependent on their atomic numbers $z^i$ and $z^j$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Moreover, the radial basis $B_u$ allows the model to scale to any number of chemical species without increasing the model size.
elements.sent-2411-054: The angular information of an atomic environment is contained in the normalized edge vectors, $\hat{\bm r = \bm r / r$, where we have omitted the atom indices $i$ and $j$ in $\bm r^{ij}$ and $r^{ij}$ for simplicity.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Unlike many existing models that expand it on spherical harmonics, we directly adopt the Cartesian basis.
elements.sent-2411-056: We define the generalized dyadic tensor of rank $v$ constructed from $\hat{\bm r}$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:dyadic} \bm D_{v} = \hat{\bm r} \otimes \hat{\bm r} \otimes \dots \otimes \hat{\bm r }\quad (v\, \mathrm{of}\, \hat{\bm r}),\end{equation} where $\otimes$ denotes a tensor product.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:dyadic} \bm D_{v} = \hat{\bm r} \otimes \hat{\bm r} \otimes \dots \otimes \hat{\bm r }\quad (v\, of\, \hat{\bm r}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: For example, $\bm D_0 = 1$ is a scalar, $\bm D_1 = \hat{\bm r = [\hat r_x, \hat r_y, \hat r_z]$ is a vector, and $\bm D_2 = \hat{\bm r \otimes \hat{\bm r $ is a rank-2 tensor, which can be written in a matrix form as \begin{equation}\bm D_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \hat r_x^2 \hat r_x \hat r_y \hat r_x \hat r_z \hat r_y\hat r_x \hat r_y^2 \hat r_y \hat r_z \hat r_z\hat r_x \hat r_z \hat r_y \hat r_z^2 \end{bmatrix},\end{equation} where $\hat r_x$, $\hat r_y$, and $\hat r_z$ are the Cartesian components of $\hat{\bm r}$ (\fref{fig:model:arch b).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\bm D_2 =  \hat r_x^2 \hat r_x \hat r_y \hat r_x \hat r_z \hat r_y\hat r_x \hat r_y^2 \hat r_y \hat r_z \hat r_z\hat r_x \hat r_z \hat r_y \hat r_z^2 ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: Any $\bm D_v$ is symmetric, as can be seen from the definition in \eref{eq:dyadic .}
elements.sent-2411-059: In \model, the angular information of an atomic environment is captured by $\bm D_0$, $\bm D_1$, $\bm D_2$, and so on, which are straightforward to evaluate.
elements.sent-2411-060: In full notation with atom indices, a generalized dyadic tensor is written as $\bm D^{ij _v$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: A representation of the local atomic environment of an atom is constructed from the radial basis, angular part, and atom feature, which we call the atomic moment: \begin{equation}\label{eq:atomic:moment} \bm M^i_{uv, p} = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} R_{uvv_1v_2} \bm h^j_{uv_1} \contract \bm D^{ij}_{v_2},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N _i$ denotes the set of neighboring atoms within a distance of $r_\mathrm{cut}$ to atom $i$.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:atomic:moment} \bm M^i_{uv, p} = \sum_{j \in N_i} R_{uvv_1v_2} \bm h^j_{uv_1} \contract \bm D^{ij}_{v_2},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: The radial part is obtained by passing the radial basis through a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), $R_{uvv_1v_2 = \mathrm{MLP (B_u)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For different combinations of $u$, $v$, $v_1$, and $v_2$, different MLPs are used.
elements.sent-2411-064: The symbol $\contract$ denotes a degree-$c$ contraction between two tensors.
elements.sent-2411-065: For example, for tensor $\bm A$ and $\bm B$ of rank-2 and rank-3, respectively, $\bm A \contractc{2 \bm B$ means $C_k = \sum_{ij A_{ij B_{ijk}$, resulting in a rank-1 tensor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: In other words, $c$ is the number of indices contracted away in each of the two tensors involved in the contraction; thus, the output tensor has a rank of $v = v_1 + v_2 - 2c$.
elements.sent-2411-067: We denote the combination of $v_1$, $v_2$, and $c$ as a path: $p = (v_1, v_2, c)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: In \eref{eq:atomic:moment , multiple paths can result in output tensors of the same rank $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For example, $p = (2, 3, 2)$ and $p = (1, 2, 1)$ both lead to atomic moment rank $v=1$.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is why the index $p$ is used in $\bm M_{uv,p ^i$ to denote from which path the atomic moment is obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The atomic moment carries significant physical implications.
elements.sent-2411-072: For a physical quantity $Q$ at a distance $r$ from a reference point, the $n$th moment of $Q$ is defined as $r^n Q$.
elements.sent-2411-073: For example, when $Q$ represents a force and $n=1$, $r Q$ corresponds to the torque.
elements.sent-2411-074: The radial part $R_{uvv_1v_2}$ functions as a weighting factor, modulating the relative contribution of different atoms $j$.
elements.sent-2411-075: This is why we refer to the output in \eref{eq:atomic:moment as the atomic moment.}
elements.sent-2411-076: By construction, the atomic moment is symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-077: This is because, first, both $\bm h^j_{uv_1}$ (explained below around \eref{eq:feature:update ) and $\bm D^{ij _{v_2}$ are symmetric; second, an additional constraint $c=v_1<v_2$ is imposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The constraint means that the indices of $\bm h^j_{uv_1}$ are thus the output atomic moment has $v = v_2 - v_1$ indices from $\bm D^{ij _{v_2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: A detailed example of evaluating \eref{eq:atomic:moment is provided in the \sifull (\sishort).}
elements.sent-2411-080: Atomic moment tensors of the same rank $v$ from different paths are combined using a linear layer: \begin{equation}\label{eq:path:mix} \bm M_{uv} = \sum_p W_{uv,p} \bm M_{uv,p},\end{equation} in which $W_{uv,p}$ are trainable parameters and the atom index $i$ is omitted for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-081: From atomic moments, we create the hyper moment as \begin{equation}\label{eq:hyper:moment} \bm H_{uv,p} = \bm M_{uv_1} \contractc{c_1} \bm M_{uv_2} \contractc{c_2} ... \contractc{c_{n-1}} \bm M_{uv_n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: Atomic moments capture two-body interactions between a center atom and its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-083: Three-body, four-body, and higher-body interactions are incorporated into the hyper moments via the self-interactions between atomic moments in \eref{eq:hyper:moment .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The hyper moments can provide a complete span of the local atomic environment by increasing the interaction order~\cite{shapeev2016moment,dusson2022atomic , which is crucial for constructing systematically improbable MLIPs.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The hyper moments are analogous to the B-basis used in ACE~\cite{drautz2019atomic and MACE~\cite{batatia2022mace .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The primary target of an MLIP model is the potential energy, a scalar quantity.
elements.sent-2411-087: Aligned with this, we construct hyper moments such that information flows from higher-rank atomic moment tensors to lower-rank ones, ultimately to scalars.
elements.sent-2411-088: Specifically, we impose the following rule: let $\bm M_{uv_n}$ be the tensor of the highest rank among all atomic moment tensors in \eref{eq:hyper:moment , then possible contractions are only between it and the other atomic moments, but not between $\bm M_{uv_1}$ and $\bm M_{uv_2}$, and so forth.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This design choice substantially reduces the number of hyper moments, which simplifies both the model architecture and the overall training process.
elements.sent-2411-090: Moreover, similar to the atomic moment, the indices of lower-rank atomic tensors $\bm M_{uv_1 , \bm M_{uv_2 , ...$ are completely contracted away, and therefore we have $v = v_n - (v_1 + v_2 + .... + v_{n-1 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This guarantees that $\bm H_{uv,p}$ is constructed to be symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-092: Multiple contractions can result in hyper moments of the same rank $v$; they are indexed by $p$.
elements.sent-2411-093: A detailed example of evaluating \eref{eq:hyper:moment to create hyper moments is provided in the \sishort.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The messages to an atom $i$ from all neighboring atoms are chosen to be a linear expansion over the hyper moments: \begin{equation}\bm m_{uv} = \sum_p W_{uv,p} \bm H_{uv,p}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: Atom features are then updated using a residual connection \cite{he2015deep by linearly combining the message and the atom feature of the previous layer: \begin{equation}\label{eq:feature:update} \bm h^t_{uv} = \sum_{u'} W_{1,uu'} \bm m_{u'v} + \sum_{u'} W_{2,uu'} \bm h^{t-1}_{u'v},\end{equation} where $t$ is the layer index.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Atom features in the input layer $\bm h_{uv' ^0$ only consist of scalars $h_{u0 ^0$, obtained as a $u$-dimensional embedding of the atomic number $z$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The message passing is performed multiple times $T$.
elements.sent-2411-099: We find that typical values of two or three are good enough based on benchmarking studies to be discussed in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-100: The atomic energy of atom $i$ is then obtained from the scalar atom features $h^{i,t _{u0}$ from all layers: \begin{equation}E^i = \sum_{t=1}^T V(\bm h^{i,t}_{u0}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Atomic energies of all atoms are summed to get the total potential energy \begin{equation}E = \sum_i E^i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: Forces on atom $i$ can then be computed as \begin{equation}\bm{F}_i = - \frac{\partial E}{\partial \bm r_i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-103: The most computationally demanding part of \model is creating the atomic moments and hyper moments.
elements.sent-2411-104: Although the need to generate atomic and hyper moments adds to the computational cost, it is still more efficient compared to methods based on spherical tensors such as NequIP \cite{batzner2022e and MACE \cite{batatia2022mace .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: To illustrate, let $v_\text{max}$ represent the maximum rank of tensors used in the model.
elements.sent-2411-106: This implies that all features $\bm h$, atomic moments $\bm M$, and hyper moments $\bm H$ have ranks up to $v_\text{max}$.
elements.sent-2411-107: The time complexity for constructing both the atomic moment in \eref{eq:atomic:moment and the hyper moment in \eref{eq:hyper:moment is $\mathcal{O (3^{v_\text{max )$ (with detailed analysis in the \sishort).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast, for spherical tensor--based models such as NequIP \cite{batzner2022e and MACE \cite{batatia2022mace , the time complexity of the Clebsch--Gordan tensor product is $\mathcal{O (L^6)$, where $L$, analogous to $v_\text{max}$ in Cartesian tensors, is the maximum rank of the irreducible spherical tensors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Empirical evidence suggests that for many material systems, using $L$ values around three yields models with good accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-110: In such cases, Cartesian tensor-based models like \model prove to be more computationally efficient.

elements.sent-2411-111: We first test \model on a dataset of inorganic lithium phosphorus sulfide (LiPS) solid-state electrolytes~\cite{batzner2022e .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Multiple models were trained using varying numbers of training samples (10, 100, 1000, and 2500), validated on 1000 samples, and tested on 5000 samples (training details in \method{sec:methods ).}
elements.sent-2411-113: Mean absolute errors (MAEs) of energy and forces on the test set are listed in \tref{tab:lips .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Compared with NequIP \cite{batzner2022e , \model yields smaller or equal errors on seven of the eight tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The learning curve in \fref{fig:learning:curve exhibits a linear decrease in both energy and forces MAEs on a log-log scale as the number of training samples increases.}
elements.sent-2411-116: These results demonstrate \model's accuracy and its capacity for further improvement with increased data.
elements.sent-2411-117: We further examine the performance in MD simulations, focusing on computing the diffusivity of lithium ions (\ce{Li+ ) in LiPS.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Diffusivity is a crucial characteristic for assessing the potential of solid materials as electrolytes in next-generation solid-state batteries.
elements.sent-2411-119: Following existing benchmarking studies~\cite{fu2023forces , we trained another model with 19000 structures with the same validation and test sets discussed above.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Five structures were randomly selected from the test set and an independent MD simulation was performed for each structure.
elements.sent-2411-121: As seen in \fref{fig:lips:results , \model accurately captures the structural information of LiPS, reproducing the radial distribution function (RDF) and angular distribution function (ADF) of the S--P--S tetrahedral angle (Fig.~S1 in the \sishort) from ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-122: It is known that MLIPs that perform well on energy and forces do not necessarily guarantee high-quality MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-123: In particular, MD simulations can collapse due to model instability.
elements.sent-2411-124: Therefore, before calculating the diffusivity, we checked the simulation stability.
elements.sent-2411-125: The stability is measured as the difference between the RDF averaged over a time window and the RDF of the entire simulation (see \method{sec:methods ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: In \olcite{fu2023forces , a small timestep of \SI{0.25 {\fs was adopted in the MD simulations using the models in \tref{tab:lips2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We also tested \model with a large timestep of \SI{1 {\fs and found that all five simulations are still stable up to \SI{50 {\ps (see Fig.~S2 in the \sishort).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The diffusion coefficient $D$ of \ce{Li+ in LiPS calculated from \model is in good agreement with the AIMD result.}
elements.sent-2411-129: After confirming the stability, $D$ was calculated using the Einstein equation by fitting a linear line between the mean squared displacement (MSD) of \ce{Li+ and the correlation time (see \method{sec:methods ).}}

elements.sent-2411-179: In this work, we develop \model, a new class of MLIP that is based on atomic moment tensors and operates entirely in the Cartesian space.
elements.sent-2411-180: Benchmarking tests on these systems demonstrate that \model achieves performance surpassing or comparable to current leading models based on spherical tensors in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and stability.
elements.sent-2411-181: It is robust and straightforward to train, without the need to tune a large number of hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-182: In all the tested systems, we only need to set four hyperparameters: the number of channels $u$, maximum tensor rank $v_\text{max}$, number of layers $T$, and cutoff radius $r_c$ to achieve good performance.
elements.sent-2411-183: As shown in \olcite{dusson2022atomic , ACE \cite{drautz2019atomic and MTP \cite{shapeev2016moment can be viewed as the same model, with the former constructed in the spherical space while the latter in the Cartesian space.}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Both are single-layer models without feature updates.
elements.sent-2411-185: Loosely speaking, MACE \cite{batatia2022mace and \model can be regarded as a generalization of the spherical ACE and Cartesian MTP, respectively, to multilayer GNNs with iterative feature updates and refinement.}
elements.sent-2411-186: The atomic moment in \eref{eq:atomic:moment and hyper moment in \eref{eq:hyper:moment are related to the A-basis and B-basis in ACE and MACE.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Moreover, \model generalizes existing Cartesian tensor models such as TeaNet \cite{takamoto2022teanet and TensorNet \cite{simeon2023tensornet that use at most second-rank tensors to arbitrary-rank tensors.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Despite these connections, \model is unique in its design of the atomic moment and hyper moment, and the selection rules that govern the tensor contractions.
elements.sent-2411-189: These characteristics make \model a physically inspired, flexible, and expressive model.
elements.sent-2411-190: Beyond energy and forces, \model can be extended to model tensorial properties.
elements.sent-2411-191: The atom features used in \model are symmetric Cartesian tensors, which can be used to output tensorial properties with slight modifications to the output block.
elements.sent-2411-192: For example, NMR tensors can be modeled by selecting and summing the scalar, vector, and symmetric rank-2 tensor components of the hyper moments, rather than using only the scalar component for potential energies.
elements.sent-2411-193: However, the current implementation of \model in PyTorch has certain limitations.
elements.sent-2411-194: All feature tensors are stored in full form, without exploiting the fact that they are symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-195: In addition, it is possible to extend \model to use irreducible representations of Cartesian tensors (basically symmetric traceless tensors), analogous to spherical irreducible representations used in spherical tensor models.
elements.sent-2411-196: Leveraging these symmetries and irreducible representations could further enhance model accuracy and improve time and memory efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-197: These are directions for future work.

elements.sent-2411-208: The models are trained by minimizing a loss function of energy and forces.
elements.sent-2411-209: For an atomic configuration $\mathcal{C}$, the loss is \begin{equation}l(\theta; \mathcal{C}) = w_\text{E} \left( \frac{E - \hat E}{N} \right)^2 + w_\text{F} \frac{\sum_{i=1}^N \| \bm F_i - \hat{\bm F_i} \|^2}{3N},\end{equation} where $N$ is the number of atoms, $E$ and $\hat E$ are the predicted and reference energies, respectively, and $\bm F_i$ and $\hat{\bm F_i}$ are the predicted and reference forces on atom $i$, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}l(\theta; C) = w_E \left( \frac{E - \hat E}{N} \right)^2 + w_F \frac{\sum_{i=1}^N \| \bm F_i - \hat{\bm F_i} \|^2}{3N},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-210: Both the energy weight $w_\text{E}$ and force weight $w_\text{F}$ are set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-211: The total loss to minimize at each optimization step is the sum of the losses of multiple configurations, \begin{equation}L(\theta) = \sum_{j=1}^B l(\theta; \mathcal{C}_j),\end{equation} where $B$ is the mini-batch size.
elements.sent-2411-212: The \model model is implemented in PyTorch~\cite{paszke2019pytorch and trained with PyTorch Lightning~\cite{lightning .}}
elements.sent-2411-213: We trained all models using the Adam optimizer~\cite{kingma2014adam with an initial learning rate of 0.01, which is reduced by a factor of 0.8 if the validation error does not decrease for 100 epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The training batch size and allowed maximum number of training epochs vary for different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-215: Training is stopped if the validation error does not reduce for a certain number of epochs (see Table~S3 in the \sishort).
elements.sent-2411-216: We use an exponential moving average with a weight 0.999 to evaluate the validation set as well as for the final model.

elements.sent-2411-222: The diffusivity can be computed from MD simulations via the Einstein equation, which relates the MSD of particles to their diffusion coefficient: \begin{equation}D = \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty} \frac{\left \langle \frac{1}{N} \sum_i^N \left |\bm r_i(t) - \bm r_i(0) \right | ^{2} \right \rangle} {2nt},\end{equation} where the MSD is computed as the ensemble average ( denoted by $\langle \cdot \rangle$) using diffusing atoms, $\bm r_i(t)$ represents the position of atom $i$ at time $t$, $N$ is the total number of considered diffusion atoms, $n$ denotes the number of dimensions (three here), and $D$ is the diffusion coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-223: To solve for the diffusion coefficient $D$, we employ a linear fitting approach as implemented in ASE \cite{larsen2017atomic , where $D$ is obtained as the slope of the MSD versus $2nt$.}

elements.sent-2411-299: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have substantially advanced atomistic simulations in materials science and chemistry by balancing accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-300: While leading MLIPs rely on representing atomic environments using spherical tensors, Cartesian representations offer potential advantages in simplicity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-301: Here, we introduce the Cartesian Atomic Moment Potential (CAMP), an approach to building MLIPs entirely in Cartesian space.
elements.sent-2411-302: CAMP constructs atomic moment tensors from neighboring atoms and employs tensor products to incorporate higher body-order interactions, providing a complete description of local atomic environments.
elements.sent-2411-303: Integrated into a graph neural network (GNN) framework, CAMP enables physically motivated, systematically improvable potentials.
elements.sent-2411-304: The model demonstrates excellent performance across diverse systems, including periodic structures, small organic molecules, and two-dimensional materials, achieving accuracy, efficiency, and stability in molecular dynamics simulations that rival or surpass current leading models.
elements.sent-2411-305: CAMP provides a powerful tool for atomistic simulations to accelerate materials understanding and discovery.
elements.sent-2411-306: MLIPs offer a balance between accuracy and efficiency~\cite{huang2021initio, unke2021machine, deringer2021gaussian, wen2021kliff , making them a suitable choice between first-principles methods such as density functional theory (DFT) and classical interatomic potentials such as the embedded atom method (EAM)~\cite{daw1984embedded .}}
elements.sent-2411-307: The current leading MLIPs in terms of accuracy and stability in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are those based on graph neural networks (GNNs).
elements.sent-2411-308: TeaNet~\cite{takamoto2022teanet and TensorNet~\cite{simeon2023tensornet have explored the use of Cartesian tensors limited to the second rank to create MLIPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-309: In this work, we propose the Cartesian Atomic Moment Potential (\model), a systematically improvable MLIP developed in Cartesian space under the GNN framework.
elements.sent-2411-310: To evaluate the performance of the model, we have conducted tests across diverse material systems, including periodic LiPS inorganic crystals~\cite{batzner2022e and bulk water~\cite{cheng2019ab , non-periodic small organic molecules~\cite{chmiela2017machine , and partially periodic two-dimensional (2D) graphene~\cite{wen2019hybrid .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: These comprehensive benchmark studies demonstrate that \model is an accurate, efficient, and stable MLIP for molecular simulations.
elements.sent-2411-312: In the following, we discuss the key components and model design choices.
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{fig1-model_architecture.pdf} \caption{Schematic overview of the \model model architecture. a An atomic structure is represented as a graph. Each atom is associated with a feature $\bm h$, and all atoms within a distance of $r_\text{cut}$ to a center atom $i$ constitute its local atomic neighborhood. b Atomic numbers $z_i$ and $z_j$ of a pair of atoms and their relative position vector $\bm r$ are expanded into the radial part $\bm R$ and angular part $\bm D$. c Atomic moment $\bm M$ of the central atom is obtained by aggregating information from neighboring atoms. d Hyper moment $\bm H$ of the central atom is computed by self-interactions between atomic moments. e Hyper moments $\bm H^t$ of different layers $t$ are used to construct the energy of the central atom via some mapping function $V$. } \label{fig:model:arch}

elements.sent-2411-380: We conducted an ablation study to evaluate the impact of \model's architectural design on prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-381: The study was performed using the LiPS 100 dataset, and all reported results correspond to the test set.
elements.sent-2411-382: First, we examined the effect of the rank of the internal Cartesian tensor representation.
elements.sent-2411-383: We found that the model utilizing tensorial features outperforms the invariant model that relies solely on scalar features.
elements.sent-2411-384: When \verb|max_v| is set to 0, \model reduces to an invariant model using only scalar features, achieving an energy error of 2.12~meV.
elements.sent-2411-385: The same applies to the results below.) By transitioning to an equivalent model with vector features (i.e., \verb|max_v=1|), the energy error decreases to 1.17~meV.
elements.sent-2411-386: Further improvements are observed with higher-rank tensorial features, yielding energy errors of 0.62, 0.43, and 0.43~meV for \verb|max_v=2, 3|, and \verb|4|, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-387: These results indicate that tensorial features are crucial for achieving high model performance.
elements.sent-2411-388: Empirically, features of rank 3 appear to be a suitable choice, as demonstrated throughout this work and summarized in \tref{tab:opt:arch .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Second, we found that message passing is essential for enhancing model performance.
elements.sent-2411-390: Without message passing, \model with a single layer behaves similarly to the MTP model \cite{shapeev2016moment , achieving an energy error of 0.84~meV.}
elements.sent-2411-391: However, when message passing is enabled, the energy error improves significantly, reaching 0.43~meV and 0.45~meV for two and three layers, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-392: Finally, we observed that other parameters have a relatively minor impact compared to the two factors discussed above, consistent with existing findings.
elements.sent-2411-393: A cutoff distance in the range of $5\sim6$~\AA\ results in energy errors around 0.43~meV, whereas a smaller value, such as 4~\AA, leads to a significantly higher energy error of 1.06~meV.
elements.sent-2411-394: The number of radial channels (\verb|max_u|) shows diminishing returns once it exceeds 16, with an energy error of 0.43~meV.
elements.sent-2411-395: Similarly, the model's performance saturates when the embedding dimension for chemical species (\verb|max_chebyshev_degree|) exceeds 8.. \def\bibsection{.}
